Name: InformationTheoreticAdaptiveCompression
Title: Information-Theoretic Adaptive Compression for Latency-Optimized Inference
Short Hypothesis: Can we design an adaptive compression scheme that dynamically allocates bits to model parameters based on their task-relevant information content, thereby optimizing inference latency without compromising accuracy?
Related Work: Recent works in model compression and optimization have explored various methods to reduce the computational and memory footprint of deep learning models. Adaptive Gradient Compression (AGC) [3] uses entropy and Fisher information to filter and prune gradients, achieving significant reductions in communication overhead. However, these methods often lack a formal information-theoretic foundation that links compression to task performance. Our proposal builds on this by integrating rate-distortion theory to dynamically allocate bits to model parameters based on their information content, providing a principled approach to latency-optimized inference.
Abstract: We propose Information-Theoretic Adaptive Compression (ITAC), a framework that dynamically allocates bits to model parameters during inference based on their task-relevant information content. By treating model parameters as a communication channel and using rate-distortion theory, ITAC ensures that the most informative parameters are preserved while reducing the overall bit rate. We derive a theoretical bound on the distortion introduced by compression and show how this bound can be used to optimize latency without significant loss in accuracy. Experiments on a variety of tasks, including image classification and natural language processing, demonstrate that ITAC achieves superior latency-accuracy trade-offs compared to static and heuristic compression methods.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Baseline Comparison - Compare ITAC against static and heuristic compression methods (e.g., uniform quantization, magnitude-based pruning) on a standard image classification task (CIFAR-10) using a ResNet-18 model. Measure inference latency and accuracy to establish a baseline.

Experiment 2: Information Content Analysis - Analyze the information content of model parameters using mutual information and entropy estimators. Identify the parameters that carry the most task-relevant information and those that can be compressed without significant loss.

Experiment 3: Dynamic Bit Allocation - Implement ITAC by dynamically allocating bits to parameters based on their information content. Use a rate-distortion trade-off to determine the optimal bit allocation. Evaluate the impact on inference latency and accuracy.

Experiment 4: Robustness to Distributional Shifts - Test ITAC's performance under distributional shifts by evaluating it on out-of-distribution (OOD) data (e.g., CIFAR-100, ImageNet). Measure the stability of the compression scheme and the robustness of the model to unseen data.

Experiment 5: Scalability and Generalization - Evaluate ITAC on a larger model (e.g., BERT) and a more complex task (e.g., question answering). Assess the generalizability of the method across different architectures and tasks.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The entropy and mutual information estimators used in ITAC may introduce biases or variances, especially in high-dimensional spaces. Careful calibration and validation are required.
Dynamic bit allocation can be computationally expensive, potentially offsetting the latency gains. Efficient algorithms for real-time information content estimation are necessary.
The theoretical bounds derived from rate-distortion theory may not always hold in practice, especially for highly non-linear models. Empirical validation is crucial.
The method's performance may vary across different tasks and architectures, requiring task-specific tuning. Generalization to new tasks and models needs further investigation.
